ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for fish salad with customisable toppings; 'salad food product' (FOODON_03301710) used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and seafood based composite dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish and seafood based composite dishes) is the closest defensible match for a fish-topped salad with mixed vegetable toppings and no sauce.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept identified for this customisable fish salad dish; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: no cooking required for salad base
fish may be grilled or poached
main_ingredients: three variable toppings (e.g. tomato, cucumber, corn, egg, carrot)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 g, representing a full plate of mixed greens topped with fish and three vegetable or protein toppings, served without sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Salad (3 toppings), No Sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Fish salad (3 toppings), no sauce' (cr_id F08010021). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this entry and has been estimated at approximately 0.4 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for comparable lean fish and mixed salad compositions (USDA SR Legacy, NDB 15261 mixed fish fillet and USDA NDB 11959 mixed salad greens). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB for this entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous evidence: mixed green salad with lean protein has a very low GI due to negligible starchy carbohydrate content (2.2 g/100g total carbohydrate, 2.0 g/100g dietary fibre leaving approximately 0.2 g net starch per 100g). Reference anchor: University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed salad vegetables (GI ~15) and lean fish (GI not applicable as protein food). Composite GI weighted toward the vegetable base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (32 × 5.5) / 100 ≈ 1.8, where carbohydrate per 250g serving = 5.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 328 mg per 100g; a 250g serving delivers approximately 820 mg sodium, representing 35–41% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request minimal or no added salt during fish preparation and avoid any sodium-containing condiments; the no-sauce version is significantly lower in sodium than the sauced variant, but fish seasoning alone contributes meaningfully and portion awareness is warranted.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low net carbohydrate (approximately 0.2 g net starch per 100g after fibre offset), low GI (~32), and high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.2 g/100g), high dietary fibre (2.0 g/100g), and lean protein (4.5 g/100g), all of which support postprandial blood glucose control; ensure fish is not battered or breaded, which would substantially raise the carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.66 g/100g), low saturated fat (estimated 0.4 g/100g), high dietary fibre, and lean fish protein support a cardioprotective dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content, absence of sauce, and vegetable-rich base make this dish appropriate for cardiovascular disease management; choosing oily fish such as salmon or mackerel would further enhance the omega-3 fatty acid content and cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Absence of sauce reduces sodium and additive load; fish provides lean protein and potential omega-3 if oily fish is selected; vegetable toppings contribute antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Selecting oily fish (salmon, mackerel) maximises omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit; nightshade-sensitive RA patients should avoid tomato, pepper, or chilli as toppings as solanine may aggravate symptoms in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Salad with Three Toppings (No Sauce) is a Western-style composed salad dish available in Singapore's hawker centres and cafes, consisting of freshly prepared fish — which may be grilled, poached, or pan-seared depending on the vendor — served over a bed of fresh mixed salad greens with a choice of three additional toppings such as cherry tomatoes, cucumber slices, hard-boiled egg, corn kernels, shredded carrot, or beetroot. The dish is served without any dressing or sauce, making it one of the lowest-calorie and lowest-sodium options in the customisable salad category. At only 42 kcal per 100g, it is extremely low in energy density. The macronutrient profile is dominated by lean protein (4.5 g/100g) and dietary fibre (2.0 g/100g), with very low carbohydrate (2.2 g/100g) and minimal fat (1.66 g/100g). The sodium content of 328 mg/100g is the primary clinical consideration, arising from fish seasoning rather than sauce. The dish has a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 32 and a glycaemic load of under 2 per serving, making it highly suitable for blood glucose management. It is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and weight control. The texture is light and fresh, with the fish providing a savoury, mildly seasoned contrast to the crisp raw vegetables. This dish is classified as halal and gluten-free in its standard preparation, though allergen status depends on the specific fish species used. Known in Chinese as 鱼肉沙拉（三种配料，无酱汁）and in Malay as Salad Ikan (3 Topping), Tanpa Sos.

How many calories in fish salad no sauce?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this exact dish (cr_id F08010021, confidence 0.95 in source). Saturated fat is estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for comparable lean fish and mixed salad greens and is clearly documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented. All other fields set to -1 are genuinely unavailable in the HPB source for this entry.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.4 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable lean fish fillet and mixed salad greens; not directly reported by HPB for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these values are not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this specific composite dish entry, and no sufficiently close single-food equivalent exists in USDA FoodData Central to reliably impute the full micronutrient profile of this customisable multi-ingredient dish. 3. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 4. This dish is catalogued in the HPB Singapore Foodo database under an English name and is served in Singapore's Western-style hawker and cafe context. Local-language names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been provided as functional descriptive translations to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Singapore-served dishes: Chinese Simplified: 鱼肉沙拉（三种配料，无酱汁）; Chinese Traditional: 魚肉沙拉（三種配料，無醬汁）; Malay: Salad Ikan (3 Topping), Tanpa Sos; Tamil: மீன் சாலட் (3 டாப்பிங்கள்), சாஸ் இல்லாமல்; Hindi: मछली सलाद (3 टॉपिंग), बिना सॉस के. These are descriptive translations rather than established culinary names in those languages. 5. The 'keto' dietary tag from the input has been retained in the alias and context as the macronutrient profile (2.2 g carbohydrate/100g) is consistent with ketogenic dietary thresholds. 6. Allergen status is fish (mandatory); gluten-free and dairy-free flags apply to the standard no-sauce preparation. 7. Aliases consistency self-check passed: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
